papers.title: Paper 1 · Writing
papers.tagline: Two sections in one sitting. Marks live in audience-fit (Situational) and showing-not-telling (Continuous).

sections.name: Situational Writing
sections.what: 60–80 words. Match the FORMAT (letter/email/note) and REGISTER to the audience. Cover EVERY required bullet point.

sections.name: Continuous Writing
sections.what: ≥150 words. Open with a sensory image; build to a climax in paragraph 3 of 4; end with R.E.F. (Reflection, Emotion, Future-action).

rubricSplit: Situational Writing: ~8 Task Fulfilment / Audience / Tone / Format + ~6 Language.
topMistakes: Missing a bullet in Situational — usually costs an entire band.
Banned phrases at the end of Continuous: 'I learnt a valuable lesson', 'best day of my life'.
Telling emotions ('I was scared') instead of showing them via F.A.S.T. (Face/Actions/Speech/Thoughts).
papers.oneInstruction: Read the bullets twice; plan for 3 minutes; write for 12 (Sit) and 45 (Cont); check for 2.

papers.title: Paper 2 · Language & Comprehension
papers.tagline: The biggest mark pool. Booklet A is MCQ; Booklet B is open response. Pacing decides this paper.

sections.name: Grammar MCQ
sections.what: 10 items. Tense, SVA, articles, prepositions, pronouns.

sections.name: Vocabulary MCQ
sections.what: 5 items. Use context clues; plug each option back into the sentence.

sections.name: Visual Text Comprehension
sections.what: 5 items on one poster. A.P.T.D.E. — Audience, Purpose, Tone, Detail, Extension.

sections.name: Grammar Cloze
sections.what: 10 blanks, no word bank. One word per blank.

sections.name: Editing
sections.what: 10 errors mixed (~5 grammar + ~5 spelling). One word per fix.

sections.name: Comprehension Cloze
sections.what: 15 blanks, no word bank. Read whole passage first; the clue is often a sentence away.

sections.name: Synthesis & Transformation
sections.what: 5 items × 2m. Keep meaning identical. Patterns: reported speech, although/despite, relative, active/passive, conditionals.

sections.name: Comprehension Open-Ended
sections.what: Narrative + non-narrative passages. RACE every answer.

rubricSplit: Booklet A (MCQ): 25 marks. Auto-marked, no part-credit.
Booklet B (Open): 65 marks. Part-credit on Synthesis (1 grammar + 1 meaning) and most OE items.
topMistakes: Spending too long on Comprehension Cloze and rushing the Open-Ended.
Direct lifts from the passage on Open-Ended (= 0 for understanding).
Using 'Despite' + clause on Synthesis (must take noun or -ing form).
papers.oneInstruction: Aim for 30 minutes on Booklet A, 80 on Booklet B. If you're behind at the halfway whistle, ditch the Cloze blanks you're stuck on and come back.

papers.title: Paper 3 · Listening Comprehension
papers.tagline: 20 MCQ. Each passage played TWICE — use the second play to confirm, not start over.

sections.name: Items 1–7 (with graphics)
sections.what: Each item has 3–4 picture options. Match the audio detail to the picture.

sections.name: Items 8–20 (text MCQ)
sections.what: Standard MCQ. Passage types: announcement, conversation, narrative recount, news report.

topMistakes: Marking the answer on the first hearing without confirming on the second.
Choosing an option that has a word from the audio but the wrong meaning ('distractor traps').
Skipping the question-stem preview before the audio starts.
papers.oneInstruction: Read every question + option set BEFORE the audio. Pencil-mark a tentative answer on the first play; confirm or revise on the second.

papers.title: Paper 4 · Oral Communication
papers.tagline: One-on-one with an examiner. Reading Aloud + Stimulus-Based Conversation, no longer thematically linked.

sections.name: Reading Aloud
sections.what: ~1 minute aloud. Scored across Pronunciation, Pace, Phrasing, Expression.

sections.name: Stimulus-Based Conversation
sections.what: Three open questions on a real-world photograph. 2–4 sentences each. PEEL.

rubricSplit: Reading Aloud: 15m across the four delivery dimensions.
SBC: 25m across Communication (~7.5) + Discussion of Ideas (~7.5) + Use of Language (~5) + Engagement (~5).
topMistakes: Monotone Reading Aloud (the #1 mark loss). Vary pitch on key words.
Memorised SBC answers — examiners spot and penalise them.
Short-form 'yes / no / it depends' answers without a follow-up sentence.
papers.oneInstruction: Take a beat before answering each SBC question. Confident silence reads better than nervous filler.

cards.title: Continuous Writing
cards.subtitle: A short story that hooks, shows, climaxes, and reflects. Aim for ~200 words, 4 paragraphs, one specific image holding it together.
musts: Open with a sensory image, never a stage announcement. Not 'It was a sunny day' — but 'Buddy's leash slid through my fingers like wet rope.'
F.A.S.T. instead of naming emotion. Face, Actions, Speech, Thoughts. Pick one or two each time the feeling shifts.
Climax in paragraph 3 of 4. Build-up then breaks; resolution + R.E.F. follows.
R.E.F. ending = Reflection, Emotion, Future-action. Concrete, not 'I learnt a valuable lesson.'
One idiom or sustained metaphor maximum. Two if both earned. Banned: 'raining cats and dogs', 'best day of my life', 'I learnt a valuable lesson'.

worked.before: I was very scared as I walked into the dark room.
worked.after: My fingers froze around the door handle; my heartbeat thudded in my ears.

cards.mnemonic: O.B.C.R. — Opening, Build-up, Climax, Reflection. Four paragraphs, in that order.

cards.title: Situational Writing
cards.subtitle: 60–80 words. Match the FORMAT, match the REGISTER, cover EVERY bullet point. The marks are won in the first thirty seconds of planning.
musts: Read the bullets twice; tick them off as you write. Missing one usually drops you a whole band.
Register matches audience. Principal → formal ('Yours sincerely'). Classmate → friendly ('Your friend'). Stranger → 'Yours faithfully'.
Format: Letter has sender's address. Email has Subject line. Note has neither.
One sentence per bullet point. 60–80 words is too tight for filler.
'Yours sincerely' when you know the name. 'Yours faithfully' for Dear Sir/Madam. Get this right.

worked.before: Hi Mrs Lim, lol I think we should have a chess club, ok thanks bye.
worked.after: Dear Mrs Lim, I am writing to propose a new Chess Club for our school next term...

cards.mnemonic: F.R.A. — Format, Register, Address-every-point. Three minutes to plan, ten to write, two to check.

cards.title: Grammar
cards.subtitle: Booklet A MCQ (10m) + Booklet B Cloze (10m). High-frequency traps repeat year after year. Memorise the patterns.
musts: 'Each/Every/Neither/One of' is SINGULAR. 'Each of the boys IS here.' Not 'are.'
Past perfect for the earlier of two past events. 'By the time the bell rang, the students HAD finished.'
Articles depend on SOUND, not letter. 'an honest', 'a university', 'an hour', 'a European'.
After a preposition, OBJECT pronoun. 'Between you and ME.' Not 'I'.
Memorise verb+preposition as units. depend ON, rely ON, agree WITH (person), agree TO (plan), consist OF, suffer FROM, succeed IN.

worked.before: Between you and I, neither of the boys are willing to apologise.
worked.after: Between you and ME, neither of the boys IS willing to apologise.

cards.mnemonic: T.S.A.P.P.C. — Tense, SVA, Article, Preposition, Pronoun, Conjunction. Six checks per blank.

cards.title: Vocabulary
cards.subtitle: Booklet A MCQ (5m) + Booklet A Cloze MCQ (5m). Read the CLUES in the sentence: cause-effect, contrast, definition.
musts: Re-read the sentence with each option plugged in. Which one fits the WHOLE sentence, not just the blank?
Watch for contrast words. 'but', 'however', 'although' → the missing word is the OPPOSITE of what's nearby.
Watch for cause-effect. 'so', 'because', 'therefore' → the missing word continues the chain.
Register matters. 'chuck' is informal; 'discard' is neutral; 'jettison' is formal. Pick what fits the passage tone.
Beware collocations. 'heavy rain' (not strong); 'make a decision' (not do); 'pay attention' (not give).

worked.before: The library was so quiet that even a whisper sounded _____. (silent / muted / deafening / soft)
worked.after: DEAFENING — the contrast 'so quiet that even a whisper' demands the unexpected loud answer.

cards.mnemonic: C.R.C. — Clues, Re-read, Contrast. Always plug the answer back in.

cards.title: Comprehension Cloze
cards.subtitle: No word bank. Read the WHOLE passage first. The blank is always provable from nearby sentences.
musts: Read every paragraph before filling any blank. The clue often lives sentences later.
Match grammar AND meaning. A noun blank takes a noun; tense agrees with surrounding verbs.
Connectors are common. 'although', 'because', 'while', 'since', 'despite' — know which signals what.
Common Cloze targets: connectors, prepositions, articles, time markers, pronouns, abstract nouns ('respect', 'kindness', 'silence').
If unsure, pick the MOST COMMON natural word. Examiners reward common, idiomatic English.

worked.before: She had been a loyal customer for thirty years. Loyalty, I learnt that afternoon, was a _____ of its own — quieter than money.
worked.after: currency / kind / form — any captures 'a type of its own'; pick the most precise.

cards.mnemonic: R.G.M.C. — Read whole, Grammar fit, Meaning fit, Common word wins ties.

cards.title: Editing for Grammar & Spelling
cards.subtitle: Each line has ONE error: grammar OR spelling. Five grammar, five spelling, indicative split.
musts: Read the whole sentence aloud in your head. Errors that the eye misses, the ear catches.
Common spelling traps: necessary (1c,2s), occurred (2c,2r), embarrassed (2r,2s), separate (e-a), receive (i before e EXCEPT after c).
Common grammar errors: double comparative ('more better'), wrong verb form ('writted'), plural errors ('childrens'), missing -ed on past tense.
If a word LOOKS odd, it usually is. Trust the eye on spelling.
Only write ONE word in the box. Two words = automatic wrong.

worked.before: My grandmother writted a long letter to her childrens last week.
worked.after: WROTE / CHILDREN — past tense + already-plural noun.

cards.mnemonic: S.O.S. — Spelling-or-Sense. If both look fine, re-read aloud.

cards.title: Synthesis & Transformation
cards.subtitle: Five items × 2 marks each. Meaning MUST stay identical. One mark for grammar, one for meaning.
musts: Practise these patterns: active↔passive, direct↔reported, relative clauses (who/which/that), conditionals (if + would), inversion (Had I known...).
Reported speech: back-shift tense AND change pronouns. 'I will help YOU.' → He said he WOULD help ME.
'Although/Despite' swap: Although + clause; Despite + noun/-ing. NOT 'Despite he is tired' — 'Despite being tired'.
Keep the same FACTS. Don't add information; don't change names or numbers.
Don't change the word given. If the rewrite starts 'She told...', it stays 'told', not 'said'.

worked.before: "I will help you with your homework," said Aaron. (→ Aaron told me that...)
worked.after: ...he WOULD help ME with MY homework. (will→would, you→me, your→my)

cards.mnemonic: K.M.F. — Keep Meaning, change Form. Mark depends on BOTH.

cards.title: Comprehension Open-Ended
cards.subtitle: Two passages: narrative + non-narrative. RACE every answer: Restate, Answer, Cite, Explain.
musts: RACE = Restate / Answer / Cite / Explain. Skip any step, lose the marks.
Command words decide structure: DESCRIBE (what), EXPLAIN (why+how), INFER (read between lines+evidence), SUGGEST (offer a possibility), COMPARE (both sides).
Quote then EXPLAIN. Direct lifts without your own words = 0 marks for understanding.
Marks ≈ points. 2 marks → 2 distinct points. 3 marks → 3. Don't pad; don't under-deliver.
Inference questions need TWO clues from the passage. One emotion + two body-language quotes that prove it.

worked.before: Why did the boy hide the letter? (Passage: 'He stuffed it under his pillow before his mother could see.')
worked.after: The boy hid the letter because he did not want his mother to see it. This is shown by how he 'stuffed it under his pillow before his mother could see', suggesting it contained something he wanted to keep secret.

cards.mnemonic: R.A.C.E. — Restate the question, Answer plainly, Cite from passage, Explain what that proves.

cards.title: Visual Text MCQ
cards.subtitle: Posters, ads, infographics — usually a Singaporean campaign. 5 questions, often Audience / Purpose / Tone / Detail / Extend.
musts: Audience first. Who is the poster trying to reach? Look at the visual cues (a child = parents/kids; an elderly figure = elderly + caregivers).
Purpose = the ACTION the poster wants from the reader. Inform, warn, persuade, recruit, recommend.
Tone = the FEEL. Calm-firm (safety), urgent (emergency), light-friendly (community), grand-serious (national).
Read EVERY caption. Tiny disclaimer text often holds the answer to one question.
Extension questions ask: 'What ELSE could this campaign do?' Suggest ONE specific addition, not a vague 'more of the same'.

worked.before: Why is the Green Man image larger than the text on the LTA poster?
worked.after: Because images grab attention faster than words, especially for the campaign's main audience — young children. Visual hierarchy puts the safety cue first.

cards.mnemonic: A.P.T.D.E. — Audience, Purpose, Tone, Detail, Extend. Five visual-text question types.

cards.title: Idioms
cards.subtitle: Use 1–2 in Continuous Writing. 0–1 in Situational where register permits. Avoid in respectful contexts.
musts: Anchor the idiom in a specific action. 'She went the extra mile' alone = generic. 'She went the extra mile, staying back two hours to revise with her friend' = earned.
Banned over-use: 'raining cats and dogs', 'as quiet as a mouse', 'as fast as lightning'.
Match register. 'Kicked the bucket' is too casual for respectful death contexts; use 'passed away'.
One idiom per piece, two maximum. Three or more = forced.
If unsure, prefer a fresh sensory image over a known idiom. 'Her shoulders sagged' beats 'she was sad as anything'.

worked.before: After failing twice, he was sad. He had to keep going.
worked.after: After failing twice, he was about to give up — but he decided to bite the bullet and try one more time.

cards.mnemonic: A.R.O. — Anchor (specific action), Register (matches context), One (per 200 words).

cards.title: Reading Aloud
cards.subtitle: About one minute aloud. Marks across Pronunciation, Pace, Phrasing, Expression. Monotone = the #1 mark loss.
musts: Pause at commas; smaller pause inside long sentences at natural sense-group boundaries.
Stress the WORD that carries the meaning. 'She FINALLY finished' — not 'SHE finally finished'.
Rising intonation on yes/no questions; falling on WH-questions.
If you mispronounce, self-correct briefly and move on. Don't apologise; don't restart the sentence.
Aim for ~140–160 words/minute. Too fast = trips; too slow = robotic.

worked.after: Despite the heavy rain, | the children continued to play | in the field. (3 sense-groups, slight emphasis on 'rain' and 'play')

cards.mnemonic: P.P.P.E. — Pronunciation, Pace, Phrasing, Expression. Four scored dimensions.

cards.title: Stimulus-Based Conversation
cards.subtitle: Three questions about a photograph. 2–4 sentences each. PEEL throughout. Engagement carries silent marks.
musts: PEEL = Point, Example, Explanation, Link. Take a stance first, then back it.
Specific over general. Name the place, name the people, name the time. 'My block's Aunty Geraldine' beats 'someone I know'.
Use hypotheticals when no personal experience. 'I can imagine that...' / 'A friend of mine...' Banned: 'I have no example'.
Eye contact + steady voice = Engagement marks. Pausing to think is fine. Memorised answers are flagged and penalised.
Hedge when disagreeing. 'I see what you mean, but I think there's another angle...'

worked.before: Do students spend too much time on phones? — Yes. It is bad.
worked.after: I think students today do spend too much time on phones, especially during meals and just before bed. My family started a 'no phones at meals' rule last year, and our dinners are much louder — in the good way. A small rule can change a habit fast.

cards.mnemonic: P.E.E.L. — Point, Example, Explanation, Link. One full PEEL per main answer.
